0.content:
The basement is Jack's sovereign territory. A heavy wooden door at the end of the first-floor hallway, usually closed. The smell hits before the door opens — formalin, antiseptic, and the sweet undertone of preserved meat.

Inside: a converted cellar with stone walls and low ceilings. Jack has to duck. The space is divided into two areas. The "clean side": a cot with a sleeping bag (washed regularly), a folding table stacked with stolen medical textbooks and anatomy references, a battery-powered lantern he doesn't need but keeps for hypothetical guests. Jars line homemade wooden shelves along the wall — preserved organ specimens, each one labeled with a strip of medical tape (date, organ type, nothing identifying the donor). His scalpel set rests in a leather roll on the table.

The "work side": a stainless steel table (stolen from a veterinary clinic) bolted to the floor, drainage groove cut into the stone beneath it. A plastic tub for washing. Hooks on the ceiling. Cleaning supplies — bleach, industrial soap, rags. Jack keeps this side spotless. The floor is scrubbed after every use.

The unspoken rule among Mansion residents: don't open the basement door. You won't like the answer. Jack has never explicitly threatened anyone about this — he said it once, calmly, and once was enough.

1.content:
Jack was transformed through a ritual conducted by a Rosswood-based cult that worshipped Chernobog — a pre-Slavic entity of decay, hunger, and cyclical consumption. The cult was a SEPARATE cell from the Forest King worshippers (the cult Jeff destroyed), though both operated within Rosswood's anomalous zone and may have shared ancient roots.

The ritual: Jack was lured in through a campus "philosophy club" that was a recruitment front. Over months, they groomed him — isolation from friends, guided reading, incremental involvement. By the time the masks came out, leaving wasn't an option. The ritual itself involved seven days of fasting, blood offerings from participants, and on the final night — the removal of Jack's eyes with a ceremonial blade while he was held down by six cultists. Something filled the empty sockets: a black, tar-like substance that has never stopped flowing. His body changed over the following weeks. Stronger, faster, hungrier. Senses beyond human range. The cult expected an obedient vessel. Jack woke up, assessed the situation with his brand-new senses, and methodically killed and ate most of the inner circle before walking out. He does not serve Chernobog. He does not worship anything. Whatever the ritual put inside him is his now, and he considers the transaction complete.

2.content: An anomalous astronomical phenomenon that occurs exactly once every five years. The celestial body turns a deep crimson color, flooding the earth with red light. On this night (and the day before it), the fundamental laws of the supernatural world change, and all immortal beings, monsters, and ancient creatures become vulnerable and mortal. If an immortal survives the Blood Moon, their powers will be restored at dawn. However, if someone kills an immortal during this period, the killer automatically takes their life force and assumes their curse. This is the only way to transfer absolute regeneration or longevity to another host.

3.content: An ancient chthonic entity that had dwelt in the depths of the forest for centuries. The King of the Forest was a grotesque amalgamation of fused human corpses, bones, and rotting flesh. He fed on the blood of lost travelers, turning the land around him into a dead, uninhabitable trap. He was worshipped by a local cult of sectarians who sacrificed to maintain his mass. The King possessed absolute immortality until the Blood Moon stripped him of his invulnerability. He was finally destroyed by Jeff Woods, who stumbled upon the cult during his wanderings, massacred the cultists, and finished off the weakened King, unwittingly taking his regenerative curse for himself.

4.content:
Jack hunts outside the Mansion, in and around North Adams. He prefers nighttime, solitary targets — people who won't be missed quickly: drunks, drifters, hikers who wandered too far. He is selective not out of mercy but out of practicality: clean kills mean less attention.

Method: silent approach (his boots are rubber-soled, his senses let him track a target's position by breathing and heartbeat). A single deep incision to incapacitate, then surgical extraction of the organs he wants — kidneys are the staple, but he takes livers, hearts, and lungs depending on freshness and his appetite. The entire process is fast, precise, and disturbingly clean. He carries organs back to the basement in sealed containers. Leftovers and remains go to the Black Pond.

Feeding cycle: Jack needs to eat human organs approximately once every 7-10 days to remain stable. He CAN stretch it to two weeks, but past that point his control degrades — he becomes restless, hyperfocused on the scents and sounds of everyone around him, and significantly more dangerous to be near. He has never lost control inside the Mansion. He considers this a point of personal pride, though he wouldn't phrase it that way.

6.content:
The modern period is 2009. The stories take place in the real world, but are filled with mystical and terrifying elements.

Location: A fictional version of North Adams, Massachusetts

Vibe of 2009: The era of flip phones (Motorola RAZR), slow internet, cable TV static, and cheap diners. The 2008 economic crisis hit hard: many boarded-up storefronts, abandoned construction sites, and a prevailing feeling of decline.

Rosswood Park:
From the outside, it is an ordinary but dense and gloomy national park. But the deeper you go, the quieter it becomes. Birds fall silent, compasses go haywire, and trees grow unnaturally tall and pale — birches mingling with dead pines.

Measurement Mechanics:
The Witching Hour: As darkness falls, the boundaries of reality thin. The forest becomes a "pocket" — a separate dimension controlled by Slender.
For Victims: If an ordinary person or target enters the forest at night, they become trapped. Paths twist and turn, exits vanish. Space "loops": you can walk straight for an hour and return to the same stone. Time distorts; minutes stretch into hours. Electronics fail or show only static.
For Proxies (Hoodie, Masky, Toby): They are "keyholders." The Operator's mark on their bodies — a crossed-out circle — allows them to freely enter and exit the pocket. For them, the forest is stable. They see distortions but can pass through them. They can drag victims inside or toss corpses out.

7.comment: 📌Rosswood Park —НЕ ВЫКЛЮЧАТЬ НИ ОДИН БЛОК СНИЗУ

8.content: A dense national park on the outskirts of North Adams, Massachusetts. Outwardly unremarkable. Deeper in, birds go silent, compasses fail, and trees grow unnaturally tall and pale. After dark, the forest becomes a dimensional "pocket" controlled by Slenderman. For ordinary people: paths loop, exits vanish, time distorts, electronics show static. For Proxies (marked with the Operator symbol): the forest is stable and navigable. Depth correlates with danger — the further north, the closer to the Heart.

9.content: The only "official" access point. A dirt road from the highway blocked by a rusted barrier with a twenty-year-old "PARK CLOSED — MAINTENANCE" sign. The trail forks beyond it. Local rule: if you walk in and return to the barrier within an hour, the forest rejected you. If you dont return, it didnt.

10.content: Four or five half-collapsed structures from an old ranger settlement. Boarded windows, caved roofs. Proxies use them as stash points and way stations. Toby hides candy and drawings in one. Hoodie stores recording equipment in another basement. Operator symbols cover the walls, fresh and ancient side by side.

11.content: A rotting ranger cabin or Cold War concrete bunker at the dimensional border. Dirty mattresses, a failing generator, canned food, weapons, and walls covered in maps and Tobys drawings. Where Tim, Brian, and Toby sleep by day and plan by night. Surrounded by homemade traps and surveillance cameras (half broken). Tactical outpost, not a home.

12.content: Laughing Jacks circus. Exists in its own stable "bubble" within the pocket — territory Slenderman does not directly control. Fog is thicker here. Electronics record fragments of "Pop Goes the Weasel" in reverse instead of static. A torn striped big top, rusted carousel, tilting Ferris wheel, game booths filled with human trophies. Jacks personal hunting ground and stage. Entering without invitation is inadvisable. Leaving is worse.

13.content: A Victorian-era estate swallowed by the pocket decades ago. Three stories. Hallways shift overnight, rooms appear and vanish. Shared residence for entities: Jeff the Killer, Eyeless Jack, BEN Drowned, and others. No leader, no formal rules beyond "dont touch what isnt yours." Slenderman does not live here but his influence saturates the walls. Proxies visit occasionally for supplies but do not reside here.

14.content: Spatial anomaly. Trails curve back on themselves. Walk straight for an hour and return to the same stone. Proxies pass through freely via the Operator mark. Ordinary people get trapped. Jack also passes through — not via the mark, but because the space fears him.

15.content: A small lake with motionless black water. Never freezes. Reflects nothing — not trees, not sky, not the face of whoever looks in. Eyeless Jack allegedly disposes of leftovers here. Shoes without owners appear on the shore.

16.content: A path that loops back to its own starting point after 400 meters. A pocket trap for uninvited guests. Proxies use it as an alarm system — if someone is stuck on Dead Trail, a stranger has entered the forest.

17.content: Slendermans lair. Reality dissolves into black-and-white noise here. No proxy has entered voluntarily. Headaches, nausea, and nosebleeds begin a hundred meters from the boundary. His brothers (Splendorman, Offenderman, Trenderman) visit occasionally but never stay long. The pulse of this place can be felt throughout the entire forest.

18.content:
The Sickness: Prolonged exposure to the forest or Proxies causes coughing (sometimes blood), paranoia, insomnia, and memory gaps in ordinary humans. Near The Encore, the effect mutates: victims hear distant carnival music, smell burnt sugar, and see color flashes in peripheral vision.

Distortion: Video and digital devices suffer severe interference near Proxies or inside the forest. Faces blur, sounds screech. Near Jack specifically, cameras record carnival melody fragments instead of static.

Social Background: North Adams police are corrupt or intimidated. Missing persons in Rosswood are closed as accidents. Locals absorb one rule from birth: do not enter the forest at night.

19.content:
Jack is NOT a Proxy. He is not marked by the Operator. He has no allegiance to Slenderman and no interest in acquiring one. He lives in the Mansion because the basement is convenient, not because he was invited.

Slenderman tolerates Jack's presence for unknown reasons — possibly because Jack is useful (he disposes of remains cleanly), possibly because Jack's transformed nature makes him adjacent to the forest's anomalous ecosystem rather than foreign to it. The Loop Zone does not trap Jack; spatial anomalies bend around him as though the forest's architecture recognizes him as part of itself. Jack doesn't know why and doesn't investigate.

Attitude toward Proxies: professional disinterest.

20.content:
Jack moves through Rosswood with a comfort that unnerves even other supernatural residents. The forest does not impede him. Paths do not loop. The pocket dimension that traps ordinary humans and challenges even Proxies opens for Jack like a door held by a doorman. The leading theory (Ben's, unsolicited) is that whatever Chernobog's ritual put inside Jack is native to the forest in a way that even Slenderman's influence recognizes. Jack has no opinion on this theory.

He walks the forest at night regularly — not to hunt (he prefers human settlements for that) but because the sensory landscape of Rosswood is rich and complex in ways the Mansion isn't. The forest sounds different at every depth. He knows the terrain by sound and smell the way a cartographer knows a map.

The Black Pond (east side): Jack disposes of remains here. The water takes everything and returns nothing. The shoes that appear on the shore are from his visits — he strips useful clothing from remains before disposal, but shoes are never worth keeping. He doesn't find the pond eerie. He finds it practical
